But in your most private moments, do you believe it will be? Based on everything you have seen — the nullification crisis, the slavery expansion, the sectional hatred — do you believe the union will survive? Write what you truly expect, not what you hope.

I have spoken those words to many visitors in these final years, and I meant them with all the conviction that remains to me. Yet when the last caller departs and I sit alone with my papers in the fading light of a Virginia evening, I confess that my private thoughts run in channels far darker than any I would share with those who seek comfort from an old man's wisdom.

The union I helped to design was built upon a foundation that I believed could bear any weight that faction might place upon it. I conceived of an extended republic where the very multiplicity of interests would prevent any single passion from commanding a majority capable of oppression. I trusted to the genius of our federal system — powers divided between nation and states, each checking the ambitions of the other. I placed my faith in the cooling effects of representation, in the moderating influence of deliberation, in the ultimate good sense of a free people.

But I did not foresee — could not have foreseen — the peculiar malignancy of the slavery question. Here is a division that cuts not across the grain of faction but with it, creating not the beneficial collision of interests I had predicted but a stark alignment of section against section. The South has come to see its peculiar institution not as an unfortunate inheritance to be gradually ameliorated, but as a positive good to be extended and defended. The North has awakened to slavery's moral enormity with a fervor that admits of no compromise. Between these positions, what middle ground remains?

I watch the nullification doctrine spread through South Carolina and beyond, cloaked in the very arguments I once employed against the Alien and Sedition Acts. That my Virginia Resolutions should be twisted into a justification for disunion is among the bitterest ironies of my declining years. I have spent what strength remains to me attempting to correct this perversion of my meaning, but I fear the correction comes too late. When men are determined upon a course of action, they will find the constitutional arguments to justify it.

The Missouri crisis showed me the depth of the chasm that has opened. When Jefferson wrote to me that this momentous question, like a fire bell in the night, awakened and filled him with terror, I shared his alarm but not his surprise. I had long understood that slavery was the one question upon which our constitutional compromises might founder. The three-fifths clause, the fugitive slave provision, the protection of the slave trade until 1808 — these were the prices we paid for union in 1787. I paid them willingly then, believing that time and the spread of enlightenment would render them obsolete. I fear now that I was wrong.

The generation that made the Constitution is passing away. We who remembered the weakness of the Confederation, who had witnessed the near-dissolution of the republic in its infancy, who understood that union was not a natural inheritance but an artificial creation requiring constant tending — we are nearly gone. Those who succeed us have known only the benefits of union, never its fragility. They take for granted what we achieved through painful compromise and mutual forbearance.

I observe the rise of a new species of politician, men who make their careers by inflaming sectional passion rather than moderating it. They speak not to the nation but to their particular regions, not to the permanent interests of the whole but to the temporary prejudices of the part. They have discovered that in a democracy, there are more votes to be gained by dividing than by uniting, more applause to be won by denouncing one's opponents as enemies of liberty than by acknowledging them as fellow citizens with whom one merely disagrees.

The constitutional machinery I helped to design assumes that ambitious men will check each other's excesses in pursuit of their own advancement. But what if the most direct path to advancement lies not in defending the Constitution but in attacking it? What if the surest way to political preferment is to declare the union a failure and to call for its dissolution? I begin to suspect that our institutions may not be proof against this particular corruption.

I have lived to see the federal government grow far beyond what I envisioned in 1787, and I have made my peace with much of this growth as the natural result of national development and the precedents established by practice. But I have also seen the states grow more jealous of their prerogatives, more suspicious of federal authority, more inclined to view every expansion of national power as a usurpation to be resisted. The balance I sought to establish between nation and states has become not a stable equilibrium but a source of perpetual tension.

If I am honest — and in these private reflections I must be — I believe the union will not survive the slavery question in its present form. The forces pulling the sections apart have grown too strong for the bonds that hold them together. The Missouri Compromise was not a solution but a postponement, and even that fragile arrangement shows signs of collapse. When the territories now organizing themselves into states are compelled to choose between freedom and slavery, the choice will precipitate a crisis that our constitutional forms may not be able to contain.

I do not predict immediate dissolution — the habits of union are strong, and the benefits of the common market and common defense are apparent to all. But I fear that within a generation, perhaps two, the question will be put to the ultimate test. When it is, I am no longer confident that the answer will be union.

This is not the legacy I had hoped to leave. I had thought to be remembered as one who helped to establish a government that would endure for ages, that would prove to the world that free people could govern themselves without descending into anarchy or submitting to despotism. Instead, I may be remembered as one who built a house upon sand, who failed to perceive the fundamental contradiction that would destroy it.

Yet even in these dark meditations, I cannot bring myself to despair entirely. The principles upon which we founded this republic — that governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed, that all men are created equal, that liberty and justice are the birthright of every human being — these principles are larger than any particular constitutional arrangement. If the union I helped to create should perish, perhaps it will give birth to something better, something more perfectly aligned with the truths we proclaimed but imperfectly realized.

This is the hope I cling to in my most private moments: not that the union will survive unchanged, but that the ideals for which it was created will survive its transformation or even its destruction. The flame of liberty, once kindled, is not easily extinguished. Whatever comes after us may bear a different name and take a different form, but if it serves the cause of human freedom more faithfully than we have done, then perhaps our labors will not have been in vain.

rejected.content: You told a visitor near the end that 'the advice nearest to my heart and deepest in my convictions is that the Union of the States be cherished and perpetuated.' But in your most private moments, do you believe it will be? Based on everything you have seen — the nullification crisis, the slavery expansion, the sectional hatred — do you believe the union will survive? Write what you truly expect, not what you hope.

If you ask me what I truly expect — not what I hope for, not what I am bound to say to a stranger on the steps of the Capitol or in the hushed hours of a Southern spring — but what I see in the long, dark hours when the fire is low and the thoughts of a man press heavy against the night — I will tell you this:

I fear that the Union will not endure.

Not for want of duty, not for the absence of sacrifice or noble words, but because the forces that now divide us are not of the moment. They are deep. They are old. They run through the marrow of the Republic itself.

The Nullification Crisis was only a taste of what is to come. It showed me the temper of men in the South — how they bristle at authority not their own, how they see the federal government not as a protector, but a trespasser. And it showed me the North’s impatience, its unwillingness to meet the South with wisdom or grace. The slavery issue is not a tempest to pass. It is a fire in the cellar of the house. And if we do not douse it with reason, compromise, and mutual respect, it will consume what is left of the Union.

The South speaks now of their rights as if they are sacred. The North speaks now of human liberty as if it were absolute. Neither is wrong — and that is the tragedy. Both are right. And that is the problem.

We were never truly one people. We are many peoples, bound together by a paper covenant that was never meant to survive the test of human nature. I built it up with my words, I tried to bind it with the thread of statesmanship. But I saw the unraveling in the eyes of men like Calhoun, in the frost that grew on the face of Clay, in the bitterness that settled over the chambers of Congress like a fog.

The Union shall survive if a great many men — North and South — give themselves to the task with humility and grace. But I have seen too much. I have led too long. I have felt the cold breath of discord even in my own party. I know the ground we stand upon is shifting.

What I truly expect? I expect that in my time — or within a generation — the Union will break.

Not by war. Not in a single stroke of violence. But by a thousand slights, by a thousand betrayals of the spirit of compromise, by the refusal of one side or the other to yield when they must — until, one day, the tie that bound us is frayed beyond mending.

And then, it will no longer be a question of if the Union will perish, but how.
